refutationAttempt: Tried to read 'big band date' as loose editorial framing for an Ellington-led session rather than a literal ensemble-size claim.
decision: The phrase 'big band date' is a factual descriptor of the ensemble, and the Personnel section enumerates exactly eight musicians (an octet), which directly contradicts a big band. The contradiction is by explicit roster, not inference.
contradictingQuote:
Duke Ellington – piano
Coleman Hawkins – tenor saxophone
Johnny Hodges – alto saxophone
Harry Carney – baritone saxophone, bass clarinet
Lawrence Brown – trombone
Ray Nance – cornet, violin
Aaron Bell – double bass
Sam Woodyard – drums
specificBadClaim: A 1963 big band date for impulse! that documents Duke Ellington's ongoing work in the idiom.

refutationAttempt: Same twin-entry attempt: read 'big band recording' as genre/era atmosphere rather than a literal ensemble size.
decision: 'Big band recording' is a concrete ensemble claim contradicted by the eight-name Personnel list (an octet). 'Hard bop period' and the blues/gospel framing are left as editorial and not part of the upheld error.
contradictingQuote:
Duke Ellington – piano
Coleman Hawkins – tenor saxophone
Johnny Hodges – alto saxophone
Harry Carney – baritone saxophone, bass clarinet
Lawrence Brown – trombone
Ray Nance – cornet, violin
Aaron Bell – double bass
Sam Woodyard – drums
specificBadClaim: A big band recording from the hard bop period

refutationAttempt: Noted the wiki lead itself contains the identical 'two bonus tracks from the same session' wording our prose copied, so tried to treat the claim as wiki-supported.
decision: The dated track-listing NOTE explicitly states the two bonus tracks were recorded on a different date (June 13, 1960) from the 1963 album session, corroborated by the personnel crediting Sam Jones on tracks 7-8 only. A specific, dated quote beats the lead's vague phrasing, so 'from the same session' is contradicted.
contradictingQuote: NOTE: "Lonesome Road" and "Smith Walk" are bonus tracks on a CD reissue that were recorded on June 13, 1960, featuring the same lineup plus Sam Jones on bass.
specificBadClaim: The album was rereleased on CD with two bonus tracks from the same session.

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat 'the title track' as a loose reference to the opener 'The Dip', which is close to the album title 'Dippin''.
decision: The MB tracklist has no track named 'Dippin''; the prose twice asserts a 'title track' (Mobley's 'most recognizable composition', a 'boogaloo-tinged burner') that does not exist on the record, and ours even lists a phantom 'Dippin'' keyTrack alongside the real 'The Dip'. The named title track is factually absent.
contradictingQuote: tracks: The Dip, Recado Bossa Nova, The Break Through, The Vamp, I See Your Face Before Me, Ballin'
specificBadClaim: built around the irresistible groove of the title track, which became a Blue Note classic

refutationAttempt: The flagged claim 'is a 1967 live in-studio album' is a verbatim copy of the entity-matched Wikipedia lead, while only MusicBrainz says 1966.
decision: Evidence is split: Wikipedia states verbatim '1967', MB states '1966'. A factual_error needs the claim to be clearly wrong, but here it is directly supported by one authoritative source and contradicted by another, so the release year cannot be settled from the provided evidence. The internal field(1966)-vs-prose(1967) mismatch is a data-hygiene issue, not a provable error in the flagged claim.

refutationAttempt: The flagged claim 'is a 1968 studio album' is lifted verbatim from the entity-matched Wikipedia lead; only the embedded MB release says 1967.
decision: Wikipedia supports '1968' verbatim ('it was not released until 1968' per its background) while MB says 1967 — the two authoritative sources conflict, so the year is unverifiable from this evidence and the flagged claim is not clearly wrong. 'Recorded in 1966' is corroborated by both.

refutationAttempt: The only contrary evidence is a German pressing's year-granularity MB date of 1967; a January 1968 US release for an album recorded October 27, 1967 is fully compatible with that.
decision: MB's '1967' is a year-only date on a DE pressing (BST 84271) and its release-group date; it does not directly contradict a specific 'January 1968' release of the original US issue. The field(1967)-vs-prose(Jan 1968) inconsistency is a data issue, but the prose claim is not provably wrong from this evidence.

refutationAttempt: Tried to discount MB because the matched release is a 2004 reissue and MB first-release dates can lag the true original release.
decision: Even granting that, the release-group firstReleaseDate is the canonical first-release year for the work and is 1967. Ours states recorded June 2, 1964 and released 'the following year' (i.e. 1965); 1967 is two years off and directly contradicts a 1965 first release, also matching ours' own year field of 1967.
specificBadClaim: recorded on June 2, 1964, in Paris and released on Blue Note the following year

refutationAttempt: Tried to read 'debut as a leader' as meaning his debut on Impulse! rather than his first-ever leader album.
decision: Wikipedia states verbatim it was the SECOND album released under his name (only the first on Impulse!), directly contradicting 'debut as a leader'. The rest (spiritual jazz framing, world-music influences) is editorial and not part of the upheld error.
contradictingQuote: It was the second album released under his name, and his first album on the Impulse! label.
specificBadClaim: Pharoah Sanders' debut as a leader after his transformative work with John Coltrane

refutationAttempt: Tried to read 'swing canon' as harmless editorial praise of a jazz-clarinet legend.
decision: The release is a classical recording — Weber's clarinet concertos performed by the Chicago Symphony under Jean Martinon, per the verbatim classical-movement tracklist and the artist credits. Calling it 'a distinguished entry in the swing canon' is a factual misdescription of what the album is, not mere opinion.
contradictingQuote: Concerto for Clarinet and Orchestra no. 1 in F minor, op. 73: I. Allegro
specificBadClaim: stands as a distinguished entry in the swing canon

refutationAttempt: The flagged claim 'released in 1969' is a verbatim copy of the entity-matched Wikipedia lead; only the MB release-group (a 1987 CD) says 1968.
decision: Evidence conflicts: Wikipedia says '1969' verbatim, the MB release-group firstReleaseDate says 1968. The release year cannot be settled from the provided sources and the flagged claim is directly supported by one of them, so it is not clearly wrong. The field(1968)-vs-prose(1969) mismatch is a data-hygiene issue only.

refutationAttempt: The flagged claim 'released on the Blue Note label in 1969' is lifted verbatim from the entity-matched Wikipedia lead; only MB says 1968.
decision: Wikipedia supports '1969' verbatim (lead and short description) while MB's release and release-group say 1968 — the sources conflict, so the year is unverifiable from this evidence and the flagged claim is not provably wrong. The field(1968)-vs-prose(1969) inconsistency is a data issue.

refutationAttempt: Tried to read '1972 session' as keyed to the release year (MB 1972-04), treating 'session' loosely as 'release'.
decision: 'Session' denotes the recording, and Wikipedia states verbatim the album was recorded in November 1971; ours' own first sentence also says 'recorded in 1971'. Calling it 'a 1972 session' is therefore factually wrong about the recording year and self-contradictory within the blurb. The 1972 year field is fine as the release year.
contradictingQuote: West Hollywood, CA on November 23 (tracks 1, 5 & 8), & November 24 (tracks 2–4, 6 & 7), 1971
specificBadClaim: a 1972 session reflecting the fusion period

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat 'A Galaxy of Love' as a loose/garbled rendering of the real closing track 'A Love Supreme' or one of the 'Galaxy...' titles.
decision: The MB tracklist names the closer verbatim as 'A Love Supreme'; there is no track 'A Galaxy of Love' anywhere on the five-track list. The prose asserts a specific closing track that does not exist on the record (and ours lists it as a keyTrack).
specificBadClaim: the transcendent closer 'A Galaxy of Love.'

refutationAttempt: Checked whether the prose makes any false assertion; it says Alice Coltrane 'delivers a focused exploration of free jazz, recorded for Columbia' and names two real tracks.
decision: Every stated fact is supported (her contribution, Columbia, the two tracks, and wiki even backs 'free jazz'). The defect is that it omits co-credited Carlos Santana, but the prose never claims the album is solo and asserts nothing false; omitting a leader/co-leader is protected editorial style and absence is not contradiction, so no factual_error survives. The artist-field choice is a cataloging convention.

refutationAttempt: MB documents only one Japanese release on CBS/Sony; that does not prove a Flying Disk pressing (a different region/imprint) never existed.
decision: Label varies by pressing and region, and MB's single matched JP CBS/Sony release does not say the album was 'not' on Flying Disk — absence of Flying Disk in this one MB record is not a contradiction. The Flying Disk claim simply cannot be checked against the provided evidence.

refutationAttempt: Tried lenient substring matching so 'Naima's Love Song' maps to 'Naima' and ignored the missing 'Fantasy in D'.
decision: The keyTracks field lists 'Fantasy in D', which is definitively absent from the five-track MB list (Bolivia, Naima, 5/4 Thing, Bittersweet, Mode for Joe), and 'Naima's Love Song', whose added words make it a different title from the album's 'Naima'. The keyTracks assert tracks that are not on this album.

refutationAttempt: Tried to read 'this 1976 session' as referring to the 1976 release/issue rather than the recording session.
decision: 'Session' denotes the recording, and both Wikipedia and ours' own first sentence say it was recorded at the Jazzhus Montmartre in 1973; 1976 is only the Inner City release year. 'This 1976 session' is therefore factually wrong and self-contradictory within the blurb. 'A document of the fusion era' is left as editorial.
contradictingQuote: recorded at the Jazzhus Montmartre in 1973 and released on the SteepleChase label
specificBadClaim: Alto saxophonist Jackie McLean's post-bop sensibility is on display in this 1976 session

refutationAttempt: Tried to find any reading of 'second Columbia album' the evidence might allow.
decision: Wikipedia states verbatim it was his FIRST Columbia album, directly contradicting ours' 'second Columbia album'. Personnel and track attributions all check out; the single contradiction is this ordinal claim.
contradictingQuote: His first album for the Columbia label, it was recorded in New York City in 1978.
specificBadClaim: Blythe's second Columbia album reinterprets jazz standards and originals

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat 'Bush Baby' as a possible alternate title for the fourth MB track 'Odessa'.
decision: The MB release has exactly four tracks and 'Bush Baby' is not among them ('Odessa' is the fourth); nothing in the evidence supports 'Bush Baby' as an alias. The keyTracks list a track that does not exist on the album.
contradictingQuote: Down San Diego Way; Lenox Avenue Breakdown; Slidin' Through; Odessa
